trace.rationale: Historical data shows that in MLB games, the first inning has a run scored approximately 55% of the time, based on general patterns from pre-cutoff seasons. No specific team or pitcher adjustments are available, so this baseline is used.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 48-52% chance of a scoreless first inning, but slight edge to runs due to average scoring trends.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 52% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific team or pitcher information to adjust this baseline.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 52% of games have a run scored in the first inning, with no specific team or pitcher info to adjust from baseline.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 52% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific team or pitcher context provided.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific evidence to deviate from this baseline.
Historical data shows that in MLB games, roughly 55% of first innings see at least one run scored, based on league-wide averages over recent seasons.
Historical data shows roughly 50-55% chance of a run in the first inning across MLB games, with no specific team or pitcher info to adjust further.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55-60% of first innings have at least one run scored, but without specific team/player context, a slight edge to 'yes' is reasonable.
